Title: Validation of the effects of molecular marker polymorphisms in LcyE and CrtRB1 on provitamin A concentrations for 26 tropical maize populations

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: 26 tropical maize populations from CIMMYT/IITA-derived backgrounds including L1-L5, H1-H15, Digenic 1-6
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Branch-point step controlling flux into the alpha-branch versus beta-branch carotenoids
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes lycopene to introduce an epsilon ring, directing carotenoid flux toward alpha-branch carotenoids such as lutein
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: competes with target-product branch by diverting flux away from beta-branch provitamin A carotenoids
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Dietary provitamin A source to alleviate vitamin A deficiency
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: High differential expression/effect reported in endosperm; not very different in embryos and not different in leaves according to cited transcript profiling discussed by the paper
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Endosperm-focused phenotypic effects in segregating kernels
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduced-function favorable alleles shift carotenoid composition toward the beta-branch and can modestly increase provitamin A, but effects are inconsistent and smaller than CrtRB1; homozygous favorable LcyE combined with favorable CrtRB1 can reduce total carotenoids
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Across five monogenic tropical populations, LcyE polymorphisms generally did not significantly increase total provitamin A. 2. In six digenic populations, the favorable reduced-function allele lowered the alpha/beta branch ratio by about 75% and increased beta-carotene, beta-cryptoxanthin, and provitamin A by about 20-30%. 3. Combining homozygous favorable LcyE with homozygous favorable CrtRB1 was not optimal because total carotenoids decreased and maximum provitamin A was often achieved with favorable CrtRB1 plus unfavorable or heterozygous LcyE.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Marker-genotype validation in segregating populations; HPLC metabolite profiling; digenic interaction analysis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key branch-control step; not established here as major bottleneck for provitamin A accumulation relative to CrtRB1
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for modifying branch flux, but should not be fixed as homozygous favorable together with favorable CrtRB1 in breeding; prioritize CrtRB1 and retain LcyE as unfavorable or heterozygous when maximizing provitamin A with minimal total carotenoid penalty
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Homozygous favorable alleles can be under-represented due to segregation distortion; combined favorable LcyE and CrtRB1 can reduce total carotenoids by about 25-30%
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Functional polymorphisms evaluated included LcyE-5'TE and LcyE-3'Indel. The paper emphasizes source species and applied species are both maize. Favorable alleles are reduced-function alleles.

Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: (KU1409/DE3/KU1409)S2-18-2-B donor background; favorable allele ultimately derived from temperate line DE3
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: 15 F3:F4 and 6 digenic tropical maize populations representing 5 genetic backgrounds
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Beta-carotene hydroxylation step in the beta-branch
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates beta-carotene to beta-cryptoxanthin and subsequently toward zeaxanthin
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: competes with target-product accumulation by converting provitamin A beta-carotene into more hydroxylated xanthophylls
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Dietary provitamin A source to alleviate vitamin A deficiency
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Discussed as showing major expression differences in endosperm, much less or none in embryo/leaves based on cited transcript profiling
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Endosperm-focused effects across tropical segregating populations
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Favorable reduced-function allele strongly increases beta-carotene and total provitamin A, typically with 2-10 fold beta-carotene enhancement and about 2.4-fold mean provitamin A increase, while reducing zeaxanthin and often total carotenoids
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Favorable CrtRB1-3'TE allele 1 had the largest effect in the study, increasing beta-carotene and total provitamin A across diverse tropical backgrounds. 2. Homozygous favorable genotypes averaged 3.8-fold higher beta-carotene and 2.4-fold higher provitamin A than homozygous unfavorable genotypes. 3. Favorable CrtRB1 reduced zeaxanthin and was associated with 21-30% lower total carotenoids, suggesting feedback inhibition or pathway-flux limitation. 4. CrtRB1 effect remained strong irrespective of LcyE background, making it the preferred marker for MAS.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Marker-genotype validation in 15 segregating populations and 6 digenic populations; HPLC carotenoid profiling; genotype-class contrasts with mixed-model analysis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for beta-carotene/provitamin A accumulation in maize endosperm; strongest breeding lever validated in this paper
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Primary marker-assisted selection target for rapid provitamin A biofortification in tropical maize; deploy favorable allele 1 widely, but avoid pyramiding with homozygous favorable LcyE when total carotenoid loss is undesirable
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Associated with 21-30% reduction in total carotenoids and strong segregation distortion in many populations; possible feedback inhibition of upstream carotenoid flux
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper notes CrtRB1 corresponds to the same locus as ZmBCH2/HYD3 in prior literature. Favorable allele is reduced-function for hydroxylation.
